turns.content: I think I need to set a boundary with my sister, but I don't know how. We talk every day and lately... I dread it.
What do you dread about it?

I think... it's that she always has something going on. Some crisis, or something she needs to process, and I'm the one she calls. And I listen, I always listen, but lately by the end of the call I feel kind of... emptied out, I guess. Like I gave something and I don't have it to give anymore.
What makes you care more about her emotional state than yours?

*pauses*

I mean... she's my sister. That's just... that's how it's always been. She's always been the one who needed more support, and I've always been the one who could give it. I think it's just how our family works.

...I don't know if I've ever really thought about it as a choice, actually. It just is what it is.
What would it feel like to let it be a choice?

*quiet for a moment*

I think... that's actually kind of a strange question to sit with. Because part of me wants to say "well of course it's a choice," but then another part of me feels almost... I don't know. Uncomfortable? Like if I call it a choice then I'm also saying I could choose differently, and that feels...

*slight pause*

I don't want to hurt her. She relies on me. I think if I started pulling back she'd really feel it.
What would you have to feel if she did?

*holds breath for a moment*

I think... guilty. Definitely guilty. Like I'd abandoned her somehow.

But also... I think I'd be afraid she'd be angry at me. Like, actually angry. Not just hurt, but... like I'd broken some unspoken agreement we've had our whole lives.

*quieter*

Which is weird to say out loud because I'm not sure I ever consciously agreed to it.
That last part — "I'm not sure I ever consciously agreed to it." Stay there for a second. What's it like to notice that?

*longer pause*

It's... a little disorienting, actually. Because on one hand I can see it clearly when I say it out loud — like, yeah, no one sat me down and said "this is your role." It just... accumulated. Over years.

But then there's this other part of me that immediately wants to say, well, but she needs me, and that's real, and I don't want to just... opt out of that because it's inconvenient.

*small exhale*

I think I'm a little afraid that if I look at it too hard, I'll have to do something about it. And I don't know what that something is. That's kind of why I came here — I was hoping someone could just tell me what to say to her so I don't have to... I don't know. Go this deep into it, maybe.

I appreciate you saying that. It's honest.

Can I just stay with one thing you said — that it accumulated, over years, without anyone choosing it?

What do you think you were getting from playing that role?

*sits with that*

Hm. I think... I was good at it. And being good at something feels... safe, I guess. Like I knew where I stood.

And I think... there's something about being needed that felt like being loved? Like, if she needed me, that meant something. About us. About the relationship.

*pause, then slightly defensive*

Which sounds kind of sad when I say it out loud. I don't mean it like I was only doing it for myself. I genuinely care about her. I do.

But I think... yeah. I think I got something from it too. I just haven't thought about what it costs now that I'm... I don't know, older. More tired. Less able to just absorb it.

It doesn't sound sad to me. It sounds true.

Being needed and being loved got fused somewhere along the way. That happens. The question that interests me now is — when you're on the phone with her, emptied out at the end, is she getting your love? Or is she getting your performance of it?
